front: What is the definition of the linear span of a set of vectors S = {v₁, v₂, ..., vₖ} in a vector space V?
back: The linear span of S, denoted Span(S), is the set of all possible linear combinations of the vectors in S. That is, Span(S) = {c₁v₁ + c₂v₂ + ... + cₖvₖ | c₁, c₂, ..., cₖ are scalars}.

front: What does it mean to say a set of vectors S spans a vector space V?
back: It means that every vector in V can be written as a linear combination of the vectors in S. In other words, the span of S is equal to the entire vector space V (Span(S) = V). S is then called a spanning set for V.

front: What is the significance of the linear span being a subspace?
back: The span of any set S of vectors is always a subspace of the vector space V. In fact, it is the smallest subspace of V that contains the set S. It is often called the subspace spanned or generated by S.

front: How is the concept of a linear span related to the concepts of linear independence and basis?
back: A basis for a vector space V is a set of vectors that is both linearly independent and spans V. The span defines the 'coverage' of the set, while linear independence ensures no redundancies. A spanning set that is also linearly independent is a minimal spanning set (a basis).

front: True or False: Adding more vectors to a set S can never decrease its span. It can only leave the span the same or make it larger.
back: True. Since any linear combination of the original vectors is also a linear combination of the larger set (by setting the coefficients of the new vectors to zero), we have Span(S) ⊆ Span(S ∪ {v}). The span can remain the same if the new vector was already in Span(S).
